title: Trauma and the Nostrils: Breathing Patterns and the Nervous System
excerpt: Explore how trauma reshapes breathing patterns through the nostrils and impacts nervous system regulation. Learn somatic tools for healing.
body:
Introduction: Why the Nostrils Matter in Trauma Recovery

When we think about trauma and its imprint on the body, the focus often lands on flashbacks, emotional overwhelm, or physical sensations. But one of the most accessible and yet overlooked pathways to nervous system regulation is our breath — specifically, how trauma impacts the way we breathe through our nostrils. Understanding this connection is vital for anyone who has tried talk therapy but still feels stuck, as it reveals the somatic root of trauma's hold.

Breathing is not just a mechanical process but a dynamic interface between our nervous system and the external world. The nostrils, as the entry point of breath, play a subtle yet powerful role in modulating autonomic states and emotional regulation.

The Science of Breath, Nostrils, and the Nervous System

The autonomic nervous system (ANS), comprising the sympathetic (fight/flight), parasympathetic (rest/digest), and the recently classified social engagement system, governs how we respond to stress and trauma. Dr. Stephen Porges' Polyvagal Theory highlights how the vagus nerve modulates these states, influencing heart rate, digestion, and breathing patterns.

Our nostrils are not passive airways; they have distinct physiological functions that impact the nervous system. Nasal breathing affects nitric oxide production, which improves oxygen exchange and has antimicrobial properties, supporting overall health (Weitzberg & Lundberg, 2013). More importantly, nostril dominance — the natural alternation between left and right nostril airflow — is connected to hemispheric brain activity and autonomic balance.

Nostril Dominance and Brain Hemispheres

Research shows that breathing through the left nostril tends to activate the parasympathetic nervous system, promoting relaxation and calm, while right nostril breathing stimulates sympathetic activation and alertness (Arambula et al., 2001). This alternation is a natural rhythm called the nasal cycle, which typically shifts every 90 minutes.

However, trauma can disrupt this cycle, locking the breath into patterns that favor sympathetic dominance or shutdown, contributing to chronic dysregulation.

How Trauma Alters Breathing Patterns Through the Nostrils

Trauma survivors often experience changes in breath that are subtle but significant. Bessel van der Kolk, in The Body Keeps the Score, emphasizes that trauma lodges itself in the body, altering physiological processes including breath (van der Kolk, 2014). Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, describes how trauma-induced hyperarousal can cause rapid, shallow breathing, often through the mouth, bypassing the nose and its calming effects (Levine, 2010).

When the nostrils become constricted or one nostril remains dominant beyond its natural cycle, it reflects an imbalance in autonomic regulation. This can manifest as:

  • Chronic hypervigilance or anxiety (right nostril dominance)
  • Depressive or shutdown states (left nostril dominance)
  • Disrupted nasal airflow contributing to poor sleep, fatigue, and heightened stress responses

Such patterns are not random but somatic signals of the nervous system’s attempts to manage overwhelming experiences.

The Role of the Vagus Nerve and Nasal Breathing

The vagus nerve is the core of parasympathetic regulation and social engagement, as highlighted by Deb Dana. It connects deeply with the regulation of breath and heart rate variability (HRV), both markers of resilience and nervous system flexibility (Dana, 2018).

Nasal breathing supports vagal tone by promoting slower, deeper inhalations that stimulate the parasympathetic system. Mouth breathing, common in trauma survivors, bypasses this mechanism and is linked to sympathetic dominance (Brown & Gerbarg, 2009).

Practices that emphasize controlled nasal breathing can help activate the ventral vagal complex, improving feelings of safety, connection, and emotional regulation.

Practical Somatic Tools: Breathing Through the Nostrils for Regulation

Understanding the science is one thing; applying it is another. Here are some evidence-based somatic tools to engage the nostrils in nervous system regulation:

1. Alternate Nostril Breathing (Nadi Shodhana)

Rooted in yogic traditions but validated by modern research, alternate nostril breathing balances sympathetic and parasympathetic activity, restoring the nasal cycle and calming the nervous system (Saoji, Raghavendra, & Manjunath, 2019).

How to practice: Using the thumb and ring finger of one hand, gently close one nostril and inhale through the open nostril. Then switch and exhale through the opposite nostril. Repeat for several minutes, focusing on smooth, calm breaths.

2. Slow Nasal Breathing to Enhance Vagal Tone

Slowing the breath to 5-6 breaths per minute with nasal inhalation and exhalation supports heart rate variability and parasympathetic activation (Lehrer, 2013).

Practice tip: Inhale gently through both nostrils for a count of 5, exhale for a count of 5-6. Notice any sensations in the nostrils and face as you breathe.

3. Mindful Observation of Nostril Sensations

Somatic expert Pat Ogden emphasizes the importance of interoception — the awareness of internal bodily sensations — in trauma healing (Ogden, Minton, & Pain, 2006). Directing mindful attention to the subtle airflow, temperature, or tingling around the nostrils can ground you in the present and interrupt trauma-triggered patterns.

Challenges and Considerations in Breath-Based Trauma Healing

While breathing practices can be powerful, they can also trigger trauma responses if done too aggressively or without support. Gabor Maté warns that forcing breath control may retraumatize by activating hyperarousal or dissociation (Maté, 2018). It’s crucial to approach nasal breathing exercises gently and with awareness, adjusting to your nervous system’s signals.

Working with a somatic therapist or trauma-informed practitioner can help tailor these practices safely. Dan Siegel’s concept of window of tolerance is key here — exercises should keep you within a manageable arousal zone to promote integration rather than overwhelm (Siegel, 1999).

Integrating Breath and Body Awareness for Lasting Trauma Recovery

Trauma disconnects us from our bodies and the natural rhythms of breath. Reconnecting through the nostrils and nasal breathing patterns offers a direct route to nervous system regulation and somatic healing.

As Francine Shapiro’s work on EMDR shows, body-based interventions complement cognitive approaches by addressing physiological imprints (Shapiro, 2018). Breathing through the nostrils is a simple, accessible tool that anyone can use to shift their autonomic state and reclaim safety in their body.

“Breath is the bridge which connects life to consciousness, which unites your body to your thoughts.” — Thich Nhat Hanh

By paying attention to the nostrils and the quality of our breath, we can begin to interrupt the cycle of trauma and cultivate a nervous system that feels more flexible, responsive, and at ease.

References

  • Arambula, P., et al. (2001). Nasal cycle and autonomic nervous system: An experimental study. American Journal of Otolaryngology.
  • Brown, R. P., & Gerbarg, P. L. (2009). Yoga breathing, meditation, and longevity. Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. Norton.
  • Lehrer, P. (2013). Heart rate variability biofeedback: How and why does it work? Frontiers in Psychology.
  • Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice. North Atlantic Books.
  • Maté, G. (2018). When the Body Says No. Wiley.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body. Norton.
  • Saoji, A. A., Raghavendra, B. R., & Manjunath, N. K. (2019). Effects of yogic breath regulation: A narrative review of scientific evidence. Journal of Ayurveda and Integrative Medicine.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy. Guilford.
  • Siegel, D. J. (1999). The Developing Mind. Guilford.
  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score. Viking.
  • Weitzberg, E., & Lundberg, J. O. (2013). Novel aspects of nasal nitric oxide: a review. Respiratory Physiology & Neurobiology.

faqs.q: How does trauma affect nasal breathing?
faqs.a: Trauma can disrupt the natural nasal cycle, causing one nostril to dominate and leading to imbalanced autonomic nervous system activity such as chronic anxiety or shutdown states.

faqs.q: Why is nasal breathing better than mouth breathing for trauma survivors?
faqs.a: Nasal breathing promotes nitric oxide production and stimulates the vagus nerve, enhancing parasympathetic activation and calming the nervous system, unlike mouth breathing which often triggers sympathetic dominance.

faqs.q: Can breathing exercises alone heal trauma?
faqs.a: Breathing exercises are powerful tools for regulation but work best as part of a holistic, trauma-informed approach that includes somatic awareness and professional support when needed.

faqs.q: What is alternate nostril breathing and how does it help?
faqs.a: Alternate nostril breathing is a practice that balances sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous system activity by consciously switching nostrils during inhale and exhale, promoting nervous system equilibrium.

affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma is stored in the body and offers insights into somatic healing approaches.

metaTitle: Trauma and Nostrils: Breathing Patterns & Nervous System
metaDescription: Learn how trauma affects nasal breathing patterns and the nervous system, plus practical somatic tools to reclaim regulation and ease.
author: The Oracle Lover
